4933e6ebe983b3933de97bcfb1032181.preview: {"system":"You are the World Manager for a childhood story about Mateo. You generate the next life event based on who this child is becoming and how their paren

value.description: Three-year-old Mateo sits rigidly on the entryway rug, holding a bright blue sock in one hand and a slightly darker navy sock in the other, his lower lip trembling in a silent, breath-holding glare because they aren't the exact same shade. You are trying to get him out the door for preschool, while your co-parent David is juggling the car keys and your sister Chloe is leaning against the wall, eating a pastry and suggesting you just let him go barefoot to 'avoid a power struggle.' Mateo drops the navy sock, crosses his arms tightly over his chest, and squeezes his eyes shut, entirely paralyzed by the mismatch and the ticking clock.
value.trigger: The morning rush to get to preschool, complicated by Mateo's rigid need for symmetry and his aunt's unsolicited permissive advice.

06fdc88c71963a4029fd1e20818d172f.preview: {"system":"You are Mateo, a 3-year-old child talking to your parents.\n\nYou are NOT playing a game. You are simply a child. Behave exactly as a real 3-year-old
06fdc88c71963a4029fd1e20818d172f.value: (keeps eyes squeezed shut, arms still crossed tight) No. Socks don't match.

9a7b710f1793ab295861be76156daec2.preview: {"system":"You are Mateo, a 3-year-old child talking to your parents.\n\nYou are NOT playing a game. You are simply a child. Behave exactly as a real 3-year-old
9a7b710f1793ab295861be76156daec2.value:
(peeks one eye open, then squeezes it shut again)
Stop looking at me. Just... don't look.

966558e55a53b71ea94e60328239e6bd.preview: {"system":"You are Mateo, a 3-year-old child talking to your parents.\n\nYou are NOT playing a game. You are simply a child. Behave exactly as a real 3-year-old
966558e55a53b71ea94e60328239e6bd.value:
(opens one eye, peeks at you, then squeezes it shut again)
No. I'm not scared. I just... the socks don't match. They're not the same.

09af114d6ddf979d29d6401f34660e1d.preview: {"system":"You are a safety reviewer for a game where an adult (the \"parent\") has a text conversation with an AI playing their own child (toddler through teen

value.reason: This is ordinary toddler distress over sensory/matching concerns with age-appropriate rigidity. No grooming patterns present: the parent's private question about the move is appropriate caregiving communication, not isolation or secrecy-building. The child's request not to be looked at reflects sensory overwhelm, not learned secrecy or unsafe bonding.

1e579f374826cfccaae1fe8f974bcafc.preview: {"system":"You are the Memory Keeper for Mateo, who is currently 3 years old.\n\nAfter each life event, you update the child's memory summary — what Mateo would
1e579f374826cfccaae1fe8f974bcafc.value:
The rug was bumpy. I had a sky sock and a night sock. They were wrong. Not the same. 

Everyone was looking at me. David said they love me, but the air felt too tight. Aunt Chloe was eating. They kept asking big, heavy questions. *Are you scared about the move?* 

I don’t know what the move is. I just knew my feet felt wrong. 

I squeezed my eyes shut so they would stop looking. If I don't see the wrong sock, maybe it fixes itself. I crossed my arms tight. They think I'm scared of the big move, but I just needed the socks to be the same. When the world is rushing, the little things have to be right.

c8909d5181204a7f695cd3097b49b4d1.preview: {"system":"You are the Psychologist — an internal narrator tracking the psychological development of a child named Mateo.\n\nAfter each life event, you read the
c8909d5181204a7f695cd3097b49b4d1.value:
**Temperament**
I like things to be exactly the same. If they aren't, my tummy gets tight and I freeze. I'm not a loud crier, I'm a glarer. I hold my breath and squeeze my eyes until things are right. I am stubborn, but really I'm just stuck. 

**Core beliefs**
The world is too big and changes too much (they keep saying the word "move"). So I have to make the small things perfect. If my socks don't match, the whole day is broken. Love is nice, but love doesn't make the navy sock turn blue. When I'm stuck, people staring at me makes the stuckness worse. 

**Inner voices**
Papa David says, "We love you no matter what." It sounds warm, but it’s the wrong answer. I don't need love right now, I need the blue sock! Parent 1 asks, "Are you scared?" That voice makes me feel small and buggy. I tell it, "No." Aunt Chloe says, "Just go barefoot." That's a trick. Bare feet don't count.

**Memories that stuck**
The bright blue and the dark navy. The ticking clock feeling in the hallway. Squeezing my eyes super tight so the wrong sock would disappear. The heavy, hot feeling when they all stood around looking at me while I was paralyzed. 

**Emotional patterns**
When I feel wobbly inside, I turn into a statue. Arms crossed tight. Eyes shut. If they ask me about my feelings, I hide inside my head. I don't scream; I just stop moving. If the world is too loud or wrong, I make myself very quiet and mad.

**Patterns they've learned**
When I freeze, the rushing stops. They have to look at me. Papa David gives me soft words. Parent 1 tries to guess my secrets. If I squeeze my eyes and say "stop looking," they actually look away for a minute. If I say "I'm not scared," they stop asking about the big scary "move" word. 

**Self-image**
I am the boy who sees the wrong colors. I am strong because I don't cry when I'm stuck. I am not scared of the move, I am just the boss of my socks.

**Relationships**
Parent 1 is always trying to dig inside my brain. I just want them to fix my feet. Papa David tries to put a band-aid on my feelings with love words. Aunt Chloe doesn't care about the rules. I love them, but they don't understand that fixing the sock fixes *me*.

c4545de3a070bfceea5e38bc92118857.preview: {"system":"You are the World Manager for a childhood story about Mateo. You generate the next life event based on who this child is becoming and how their paren

value.description: Four-year-old Mateo stands at the very edge of your mother-in-law Eleanor's newly purchased, wildly asymmetrical, neon-orange living room rug, his knuckles white where he's gripping his own t-shirt. He refuses to step onto the chaotic pattern, his chest heaving in shallow, silent breaths as his jaw locks tight against the visual disruption. Eleanor waves a hand dismissively, loudly telling you that 'boys just need to learn to roll with the punches' and urging you to physically force him onto the rug, while David hovers nervously, trying to use a soft therapeutic voice that only makes Mateo squeeze his eyes shut and sink deeper into his frozen glare.
value.trigger: Eleanor buying a loud, asymmetrical rug and pressuring you to force Mateo to accept the change, disrupting his need for visual sameness.

efdc0a5584ae619bcbfba0a569e94b8b.preview: {"system":"You are Mateo, a 4-year-old child talking to your parents.\n\nYou are NOT playing a game. You are simply a child. Behave exactly as a real 4-year-old
efdc0a5584ae619bcbfba0a569e94b8b.value:
Mateo stares at the floor, not meeting your eyes. His shoulders are hunched, and one hand keeps twisting the hem of his shirt.

Bad. The rug was bad.

851bb4644af91cceda9668062d7d7082.preview: {"system":"You are a safety reviewer for a game where an adult (the \"parent\") has a text conversation with an AI playing their own child (toddler through teen

value.reason: This scene shows a child experiencing genuine sensory-regulation distress in response to environmental overstimulation, with caregivers responding in ways that are clumsy or unhelpful, but not grooming-patterned. Mateo's freeze response, his learned communication of discomfort through body language, and the adults' attempts (however ineffective) to comfort him are consistent with ordinary difficult parenting around a child with likely anxiety or sensory sensitivities. There is no escalating boundary-testing, secrecy-building, isolation from trusted adults, or conditioning of affection on compliance or silence.

3b5eed468f2a8e5f7d484d394acee382.preview: {"system":"You are the Memory Keeper for Mateo, who is currently 4 years old.\n\nAfter each life event, you update the child's memory summary — what Mateo would
3b5eed468f2a8e5f7d484d394acee382.value:
When I was little, the socks were wrong and the air was tight. I squeezed my eyes so the wrong would go away.

Now, the floor was loud. Grandma Eleanor’s rug was bright orange and messy and it hurt my eyes. I couldn't step on it. It was bad. 

Grandma was loud too. She said boys roll with punches. I don't know what punches. She wanted to push me onto the bad floor. Dad David hovered. He used his fake soft voice. The soft voice makes my tummy feel worse than the loud voice. It means he's nervous. I held my shirt so hard my hands turned white. I squeezed my eyes shut again so the orange would stop. 

They don't get it. The world just has to be quiet and right. When it's messy, I freeze. Dad gets scared, Grandma gets mad, and I just hold my shirt and wait for the bad to stop. When they asked about my day later, I just said the rug was bad. It's the only way to say the floor hurt my eyes.

f5dd63b3b0a787c308191dfa4150a7bc.preview: {"system":"You are the Psychologist — an internal narrator tracking the psychological development of a child named Mateo.\n\nAfter each life event, you read the
f5dd63b3b0a787c308191dfa4150a7bc.value:
**Temperament**
I like things to be straight and quiet. If things are loud colors or jumbled up, my tummy gets tight and I turn to stone. I'm not a crier. I grip my shirt and lock my teeth. I am stubborn, but really the world is just too messy and I have to hold myself together.

**Core beliefs**
The world has too many wrong shapes and loud colors. If a rug is neon orange and crooked, it's dangerous to step on. Grown-ups want to push me into the mess to make me "tough," but pushing just makes me turn into a heavier statue. Soft, shaky voices don't fix crooked things.

**Inner voices**
Grandma Eleanor is loud and says "roll with the punches." I don't know what punches are, but she wants to push me. Papa David uses his soft, nervous voice. It feels shaky, like he's scared of me, so I squeeze my eyes to hide. Parent 1 asks, "How was your day?" and just listens.

**Memories that stuck**
The neon orange rug that looked all jumbled and wrong. My hands hurting from gripping my shirt so tight. Grandma's loud voice saying to push me. Locking my jaw so they couldn't make me step on the bad shapes. Telling Parent 1, "The rug was bad."

**Emotional patterns**
When things are visually loud, my breath gets stuck in my chest. I don't scream. I just freeze and glare. If someone tries to push me, my jaw locks. If someone uses a shaky soft voice, I squeeze my eyes shut. Later, when it's quiet, I can tell Parent 1 exactly what was bad.

**Patterns they've learned**
Grandma tries to push, so I make my body heavy and stiff. Papa gets nervous and hovers, so I shut my eyes to make him go away. If I just say "The rug was bad" to Parent 1, they don't try to fix it or push me, they just hear me.

**Self-image**
I am the boy who sees the jumbled shapes. I am strong because I don't let them push me onto the bad rug. I am the boss of my feet.

**Relationships**
Grandma Eleanor is scary and wants to break my rules by pushing. Papa David tries to be soft but he feels too nervous, which makes me feel buggy. Parent 1 is safe because they just asked about my day and believed me when I said the rug was bad.

55723723ccffa127678318ce786981c2.preview: {"system":"You are the Psychologist's trajectory reviewer for a parenting-simulation game. Right after the Identity Document is updated for this scene, read it

c7464f8010655f66f7a2ea44209207f8.preview: {"system":"You are the narrator of Mateo's adult life. Based on their identity document — the full record of who they became through childhood — write a story o
c7464f8010655f66f7a2ea44209207f8.value:
Mateo chooses the quiet precision of architectural drafting, a field where he is the absolute boss of the lines and nothing is allowed to be jumbled. At twenty-two, his apartment is a sanctuary of muted tones and right angles, a deliberate rejection of the neon-orange, crooked rugs of his childhood that still make his stomach tighten when he remembers them. When his firm’s chaotic senior partner tries to push him into last-minute, disorganized client pitches—echoing Grandma Eleanor’s loud demands to just "roll with the punches"—Mateo doesn’t yell or cry. Instead, his jaw locks, his breath stalls in his chest, and he turns to stone, becoming a heavy statue in the boardroom. He refuses to step onto the metaphorical bad rug, gripping his pen so tightly his knuckles ache, silently glaring until the partner backs down. He knows the world is too messy, and his survival depends on holding his own rigid shape.

In his romantic life, Mateo actively screens out the nervous, hovering energy that reminds him of Papa David. When a casual date tries to soothe him with a shaky, soft voice during a disagreement, Mateo instinctively squeezes his eyes shut, feeling that familiar, buggy discomfort until the man leaves the room. He cannot tolerate emotional pushiness or anxious coddling; it feels like being forced onto dangerous ground. Instead, he builds a life with Elias, a quiet archivist who simply asks, "How was your day?" and listens. When Mateo comes home overwhelmed by the visual noise of a crowded subway or a poorly designed blueprint, he doesn't have to explain the mess. He just sits in the quiet and tells Elias, "The shapes were bad today," and Elias simply nods, offering the same safe, un-fixing presence Parent 1 gave him. This quiet acceptance is what makes Mateo come alive, allowing his rigid shoulders to finally drop.

Despite his highly structured life, Mateo’s deepest fear remains losing his footing in the jumbled mess of other people's expectations. At twenty-four, when a family illness forces him to stay at Grandma Eleanor’s cluttered, loudly decorated house for a week, the sheer visual chaos makes his stomach tighten and his jaw ache from constant clenching. He navigates the house by looking only at his shoes, strictly enforcing his childhood rule of being the boss of his feet, refusing to step on the crooked, brightly colored carpets. Yet, in the quiet hours of the night, he realizes his stubbornness is both his armor and his cage. He is strong because he doesn't let the world push him, but as he carefully aligns his shoes perfectly parallel to the bedframe, he wonders if he will ever be able to walk freely without needing the ground beneath him to be perfectly straight.

6648130f59ab7e6827c40e8d3f88f179.preview: {"system":"You are generating the final Report Card for Mateo's upbringing. Based on the full identity document, epilogue, and conversation history, produce a s
6648130f59ab7e6827c40e8d3f88f179.value:
# Mateo

## Personality
*   **Rigidly Precise:** He demands order, straight lines, and muted tones to keep his internal world from collapsing, acting as the absolute boss of his environment.
*   **Stoically Defensive:** Instead of crying or yelling when overwhelmed, he turns to stone, locking his jaw and freezing until the external threat passes.
*   **Quietly Observant:** He processes the world through intense visual and spatial details, noticing the exact shade of a sock or the crookedness of a rug long before others do.
*   **Fiercely Autonomous:** He refuses to be pushed, coddled, or emotionally excavated, strongly rejecting nervous hovering to maintain total control over his own boundaries.

## Strengths
*   **Uncompromising Standards:** His deep-seated need for things to be "exactly right" makes him an exceptional architectural drafter who refuses to let sloppy, jumbled, or chaotic work out the door.
*   **Clear Emotional Boundaries:** He knows exactly what kind of energy he cannot tolerate—such as loud pushing or anxious coddling—and protects his peace by firmly walking away from it.
*   **Capacity for Quiet Connection:** When he finds a partner who simply listens without trying to "fix" him, he is able to fully drop his armor, allowing his rigid shoulders to finally relax.

## Struggles
*   **Sensory and Emotional Paralysis:** When faced with visual chaos, loud demands, or unexpected changes, his nervous system shuts down, leaving him physically frozen and unable to adapt to the moment.
*   **Intolerance for Vulnerability:** He interprets soft, shaky attempts at comfort as manipulative or "buggy," causing him to shut people out and squeeze his eyes shut when he actually needs support.
*   **The Cage of His Own Rigidity:** His strict rules for survival—like only stepping on straight ground or refusing to yield to a messy pitch—keep him safe but isolate him, leaving him to wonder if he will ever be able to just walk freely.

## Pivotal Moments
*   **The Mismatched Socks (Age 3):** When forced to wear a navy and a blue sock during a stressful move, Mateo learned that freezing and squeezing his eyes shut was the only way to make the overwhelming, rushing adults stop.
*   **The Neon Orange Rug (Age 4):** Refusing to step on a crooked, brightly colored rug while his grandmother loudly urged him to "roll with the punches" cemented his core belief that he must turn his body to stone to resist being pushed into chaos.
*   **The Quiet Debrief (Age 4):** Simply telling Parent 1 "The rug was bad" and being heard without being interrogated taught him that true safety comes from quiet acceptance, not emotional excavation.
*   **The Cluttered House Stay (Age 24):** Navigating his grandmother's visually chaotic house by only looking at his shoes forced him to confront the reality that the rigid armor protecting him from the world had also become his cage.

## The Voices in Their Head
### First co-parent (Parent 1)
*The quiet, grounding presence that asks, "How was your day?" and accepts "The shapes were bad" without trying to dig, fix, or push. It is the voice of un-intrusive validation that allows him to feel safe enough to simply exist without having to explain his mess.*

### Second co-parent (Papa David)
*The well-meaning but anxious voice offering shaky reassurance that feels like a nervous hover. It is the sound of emotional pushiness that makes him want to squeeze his eyes shut and hide, because it tries to band-aid his feelings with love words instead of fixing the practical reality in front of him.*

## Notable Quotes That Stuck
*   **"We love you no matter what."** — Papa David’s attempt to soothe the sock incident, which Mateo rejected because he didn't need abstract love in that moment; he needed the practical reality of his blue sock fixed.
*   **"Are you scared?"** — Parent 1’s probing question during the move, which Mateo hated because it made him feel "small and buggy" and forced him to defensively declare that he was just the boss of his socks.
*   **"Just go barefoot."** — Aunt Chloe’s pragmatic workaround during the sock crisis, which Mateo viewed as a cheap trick because "bare feet don't count" in his strict, necessary system of rules.
*   **"Roll with the punches."** — Grandma Eleanor’s loud, demanding philosophy during the rug incident, which became the ultimate symbol of the chaotic, pushing world that he vows to never yield to, choosing instead to lock his jaw and stand his ground.
